sources.full: Guild Wars Eye of the North

adventure.name: Guild Wars Eye of the North

contents.name: Depths of Tyria Part 1
headers: I Feel the Earth Move Under Cantha's Feet

entries: The Guild Wars franchise is the flagship franchise of the Washington state based development studio ArenaNet.
The fourth entry into it's now 20 year old story is Guild Wars Eye of the North.
This book aims to capture the story and the feelings of this beloved game, and bring them to the gaming table with the help of the Dungeons & Dragons 5 edition rule set.
All credit goes to the amazing people at ArenaNet and NCSoft, who have given us players such magical adventures.
Introduction

action.name: Rifle Shot
entries: {@atk rw} {@dice #$prompt_number:min=1,title= Your spell attack modifier!,default=5$#|Bonus equals your spell attack modifier}, range 60/240 ft., one target. {@h} {@dice 1d10 + #$prompt_number:min=1,title= Your proficiency bonus!,default=2$#|1d6 + your proficiency bonus} piercing damage.

spell.name: Aura of Stability
entries: {@variantrule Enchantment|GW}
Give a creature a short burst of divine stability.
Choose one creature, other than yourself, that you can see within range. For the duration, the target is immune to the {@condition prone} condition.
Aura of Stability
Enchantment Spell. For 3...7...8 seconds, target other ally cannot be knocked down.

entriesHigherLevel.name: Cantrip Upgrade
entries: The number of targets increases by 1 when you reach levels 5 (2), 11 (3), and 17 (4).

background.name: Charr Homelands
entries: The Charr Homelands lie to the north of Ascalon and, as the name implies, is the current homeland of the Charr. Its geography is much like how Ascalon was before the Searing and is home to Grawl, Skale, Worms, and a variety of Devourers among other creatures. Its true name is unknown.
The Elder Dragon Kralkatorrik can be seen sleeping in the center of this region, between the three explorable areas.

background.name: Depths of Tyria
entries: The Depths of Tyria, sometimes simply called the Depths, are large networks of underground caverns and tunnels underneath the Tyrian surface, filled with a variety of different structures of unknown origin, ranging from structures similar to Ascalonian buildings to statues akin to those found in the Echovald Forest and unique crystalline structures.

Many of the races found in the Depths of Tyria are pale compared to their surface counterparts and are, on occasion, larger as well. The Depths are home to Arachnids, Skelks, Fleshreavers, Oozes, and is the origin of the Asura.

background.name: Far Shiverpeaks
entries: The Far Shiverpeaks is the northernmost section of the Shiverpeak Mountains that, until 1078 AE and the Destroyer threat, was unexplored and unknown to most Tyrians. It is the home of the individualistic Norn, the gigantic Jotun, and the Centaurs of the Shiverpeaks, the Modniir.

background.name: Tarnished Coast
entries: Previously uninhabited, the Tarnished Coast is the coast of the Maguuma Jungle. It is where the Asura now reside, forced from the Depths of Tyria by the Destroyers. It currently acts as their staging ground for their stand against the Great Destroyer. It is full of ruins that have been renovated by the Asura, and is home to ground that is brimming with magical energies, and in turn levitates. Some of this magic is harnessed for the Golems.

The architecture of the Tarnished Coast is a combination of preexisting architecture with Asuran modifications. It is unknown which race or civilization built the architecture, but one thing is for sure, there was once a civilization situated in the Tarnished Coast before the Asura arrived there.

entries.name: Darkvision
entries: You have {@sense Darkvision} with a range of 60 feet.

entries.name: Keen Senses
entries: You have proficiency in the {@skill Insight}, {@skill Perception} or {@skill Survival} skill.

entries.name: Charr Legion
entries: You are part of a Charr Legion grants you special traits. Choose a Legion from the Charr Legion table. You gain the level 1 benefit of that legion. When you reach levels 3 and 5, your gain additional benefits.

If you learn a spell through your legion the following rules apply: You always have that spell prepared. You can cast it once without a spell slot, and you regain the ability to cast it in that way when you finish a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}. You can also cast the spell using any spell slots you have of the appropriate level. Intelligence, Wisdom, or Charisma is your spellcasting ability for the spells you cast with this trait (choose the ability when you select the lineage).

entries.name: Charr Legion
rows: Ash
You have proficiency in the {@skill Stealth} skill. If you already have proficiency, choose a different skill to have proficiency in.

When attacking while {@condition Invisible} or attacking a creature that is {@condition Surprised}, your attack rolls with weapons and Unarmed Strikes can score a {@variantrule Critical Hit|XPHB} on a roll of 19 or 20 on the {@dice d20}. If you already have a feature that increases your critical hit range, increase it by one instead.
You can take the {@action Hide|XPHB} action as a {@variantrule Bonus Action|XPHB}.
Blood
You have proficiency in the {@skill Athletics} skill. If you already have proficiency, choose a different skill to have proficiency in.

Once per turn, when you deal damage to a creature that is {@condition Bloodied|XPHB}, you can deal addition damage to that creature. The additional damage equals your {@table Proficiency Bonus|XPHB} and is the same damage type as the damage that triggered it. You can use this feature a number of times equal to your {@table Proficiency Bonus|XPHB}, and you regain all expended uses when you finish a {@variantrule Short Rest|XPHB|Short} or {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}.

When you reduce a creature to 0 {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB} with an attack, you can take a {@variantrule Bonus Action|XPHB} to move up to half your {@variantrule Speed|XPHB} and make one attack with a weapon or an Unarmed Strike. You can use this feature a number of times equal to your {@table Proficiency Bonus|XPHB}, and you regain all expended uses when you finish a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}.
Flame
You learn the {@spell Flare|GW} spell. You have proficiency in the {@skill Arcana} skill. If you already have proficiency, choose a different skill to have proficiency in.

You learn the {@spell Fireball (Guild Wars)|GW} spell. You have {@variantrule Resistance|XPHB} to Fire damage. When you deal Bludgeoning, Piercing, or Slashing you can choose to deal Fire damage instead.
You learn the {@spell Meteor|GW} spell. Fire damage you deal ignores {@variantrule Resistance|XPHB|Resistances}.
Iron
You have proficiency with {@item Tinker's Tools|XPHB}. If you already have proficiency, choose a different tool to have proficiency in.

As a {@action Magic|XPHB} action you can create a {@creature Iron Legion Turret|GWEotN} in an unoccupied space next to you for 1 hour. The turret is an ally to you and your allies. In combat, it share you {@variantrule Initiative|XPHB} count, but it takes its turn immediately after yours. It obeys your verbal commands (no action required by you). If you don't issue any, it takes the {@action Dodge|XPHB} action. You can use this feature a number of times equal to your {@table Proficiency Bonus|XPHB}, and you regain all expended uses when you finish a {@variantrule Short Rest|XPHB|Short} or {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}.
When you create your {@creature Iron Legion Turret|GWEotN} you can now create two at once, using a use of this feature for each.

entries: The Charr are aggressive, industrious, and wholly dedicated to becoming the preeminent species of Tyria. A savage race of sentient beasts, the Charr will use any means available to destroy their enemies: an ambush is as honorable as a fair fight, as victory is what matters. Though Charr have no concept of mercy, they can be protective of those within their warband.

The Charr are a savage fighting race that once dominated the eastern side of the Shiverpeak Mountains. They fought anything they could - including themselves. Eventually the many warbands of the Charr united under a single leader: the Khan-Ur.

In the days before the Gods of Tyria brought the Humans to Tyria, the Charr battled with the Forgotten in the Blazeridge Mountains. Long after the Forgotten stopped threatening the Charr, the Humans appeared and, with the help of their Gods, pushed the Charr from their southern borders, conquering the land that became known as Ascalon in 100 BE. Along with being pushed back, the Khan-Ur was assassinated, preventing any hope the Charr had to retake their lands. The death of the Khan-Ur caused a schism among his four children, who became known as imperators, and brought the establishment of the High Legions - the Ash Legion, the Blood Legion, the Flame Legion, and the Iron Legion.

In order to combat the Humans, they sought gods to call their own, and at the volcano called Hrangmer, the Burnt Warband discovered gods for the Charr. Having found gods, the Shaman caste, the first Charr to unite under the new fiery gods, united the rest of the Charr warbands under their faith. Though one Charr, Bathea Havocbringer, rejected these new gods. She was quickly executed as a living sacrifice to the gods and the Shaman caste banned all female Charr from warbands and armies, sent to do utility work.

entries.name: Ash
entries: The Ash Legion is one of the four High Legions of the charr, famed for their spies, stalkers and assassins. They were formed by the Khan-Ur's spymaster, one of the Khan-Ur's children.

entries.name: Blood
entries: The Blood Legion is one of the four High Legions in charr society. They are known for their prowess in traditional battlefield combat. The Blood Legion controls the largest known amount of territory of the charr, having both the Blood Legion Homelands as well as the lands on the eastern side of the Blazeridge Mountains.

entries.name: Flame
entries: The Flame Legion is one of the four High Legions of the Charr. The Flame Legion is renowned for its members' magical prowess and is most commonly associated with the Shaman caste.

To the Charr, flame is the physical representation of their gods. In order to keep the gods happy, they must first fulfil their obligation to the flame. To that end, the Charr have constructed a series of sacred buildings known as Flame Temples. On raised platforms, these bestial creatures have inscribed huge circular runes, which define and magically feed the sacred fires. Further defining the ring are pillars of pure obsidian or carved sandstone, sculpted to depict the faces of the mysterious creatures the Charr worship. Between these pillars, in the very centre of each of these temples, are the ever-burning Sacred Flames. The Charr believe that as long as the flames are burning, the gods are pleased. If the flames go out, then the Charr have failed in their duties and will be punished by the gods.

Each temple has its own fire, which is tended at all times by four Charr Flame Keepers. The Flame Keepers have only one job—to keep the Sacred Flame burning. This is the most prestigious position a Charr can occupy; only the strongest, most capable Charr can take this honour, and all other Charr bow to the Flame Keepers.

In addition to the temples, whenever a large Charr warband travels anywhere, they take with them a bronze brazier, lit at a Flame Temple by the Sacred Flame. These braziers are placed upon palanquins, which are then carried from location to location by four Charr Flame Carriers. Each night when they make camp, the Flame Carriers erect a flammable effigy in the shape of one of their gods, then ignite it and let it burn till daylight.

entries.name: Iron
entries: The Iron Legion is one of the four High Legions of the Charr. The Iron Legion is known for its Siege Devourers, catapults, towers, and other mechanical works.

entries.name: Cold Climate Adaptation
entries: You have {@variantrule Resistance|XPHB} to Cold damage. Moving through {@variantrule Difficult Terrain|XPHB} caused by ice or snow costs you no extra movement.

entries.name: Norn Spirit Animal
entries: You revere a specific Spirit Animal that grants you special traits. Choose a Spirit Animal from the Norn Spirit Animal table. You gain the level 1 benefit of that spirit animal. When you reach levels 3 and 5, your gain additional benefits.

If you learn a spell through your spirit animal the following rules apply: You always have that spell prepared. You can cast it once without a spell slot, and you regain the ability to cast it in that way when you finish a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}. You can also cast the spell using any spell slots you have of the appropriate level. Intelligence, Wisdom, or Charisma is your spellcasting ability for the spells you cast with this trait (choose the ability when you select the lineage).

entries.name: Norn Spirit Animal
rows: Bear
Your {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB|Hit Point} maximum increases by 1, and it increases by 1 again whenever you gain a level.
You learn the {@spell Polymorph|XPHB} spell. You can only target yourself with it, and you can only transform into Ursidae.
You have proficiency in Strength, Dexterity or Constitution saving throws (choose the proficiency when you select the lineage).
Dragon
You have {@variantrule Resistance|XPHB} to Acid, Fire, Lightning, Poison, or Thunder damage (choose the resistance when you select the lineage).
You learn the {@spell Polymorph|XPHB} spell. You can only target yourself with it, and you can only transform into Squamata.
While {@variantrule Shape-Shifting|XPHB|Shape Shifted} into a Squamata you have a {@variantrule Fly Speed|XPHB} equal to your {@variantrule Speed|XPHB}.
Owl
You have proficiency in the {@skill Perception} skill. If you already have proficiency, choose a different skill to have proficiency in.
You learn the {@spell Polymorph|XPHB} spell. You can only target yourself with it, and you can only transform into Strigiformes.

When you fail a Wisdom {@variantrule D20 Test|XPHB} you can expend a {@variantrule Hit Point Dice|XPHB}. Rather then regaining {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB}, you roll the {@variantrule Hit Point Dice|XPHB} and add the number rolled to the test, potentially turning it into a success. If the test still fails, you regain your {@variantrule Hit Point Dice|XPHB}.
Raven
You have proficiency in the {@skill Investigation} skill. If you already have proficiency, choose a different skill to have proficiency in.
You learn the {@spell Polymorph|XPHB} spell. You can only target yourself with it, and you can only transform into Corvidae.

When you fail a Intelligence {@variantrule D20 Test|XPHB} you can expend a {@variantrule Hit Point Dice|XPHB}. Rather then regaining {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB}, you roll the {@variantrule Hit Point Dice|XPHB} and add the number rolled to the test, potentially turning it into a success. If the test still fails, you regain your {@variantrule Hit Point Dice|XPHB}.
Snow Leopard
You have proficiency in the {@skill Stealth} skill. If you already have proficiency, choose a different skill to have proficiency in.
You learn the {@spell Polymorph|XPHB} spell. You can only target yourself with it, and you can only transform into Panthera.

When you fail a Dexterity {@variantrule D20 Test|XPHB} you can expend a {@variantrule Hit Point Dice|XPHB}. Rather then regaining {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB}, you roll the {@variantrule Hit Point Dice|XPHB} and add the number rolled to the test, potentially turning it into a success. If the test still fails, you regain your {@variantrule Hit Point Dice|XPHB}.
Wolf
Your {@variantrule Speed|XPHB} increases to 35 feet.
You learn the {@spell Polymorph|XPHB} spell. You can only target yourself with it, and you can only transform into Canis Lupus.

You have {@variantrule Advantage|XPHB} on an attack against a creature if at least one of your allies is within 5 feet of the creature and the ally doesn't have the {@condition Incapacitated|XPHB} condition.

entries: The Norn are a race of nine-foot-tall warriors who live in the northernmost Shiverpeaks. They revel in the harsh climes, leading dangerous lives among savage beasts. These mighty hunters are not organized into a single nation. Instead, those who display exceptional strength and prowess in battle establish homesteads, though they are certainly not considered 'rulers'. They often spend years at a time tracking a particularly strong or clever quarry; they never give up on a battle, a pursuit, or a friend.

The Norn revere the spirits of nature—from the wolf to the snow lynx -- but the most powerful of these is the Bear Spirit, who, according to myth, blessed the Norn with the ability to change shape and 'become the bear'.

A race of shape-shifting half-giants from the north, the Norn value personal strength, victory, and purifying the soul through battle. They revere the spirits of animals and call on them like shamans.

The Norn are a highly independent race with a fighter-centric society, holding strength and prowess in combat above everything else. To a Norn, the greatest shame is not dying in battle, but rather not having the tale of their life, especially death, told. Due to their independence, the Norn are hardly seen in large parties with the exception of great hunts, festivities, and in the larger homesteads like Gunnar's Hold.

For the Norn, in order to wed, the couple must be of equal reputation, and marriage symbolizes the combining of their tales. During the wedding, someone must speak on the behalf of the two to show their worth for the other.

Norn culture is highly individualistic with a focus on personal success and glory—particularly when gained from the hunt or in a battle. The norn do not fear death as they believe the bold and strong can achieve immortality, but they do fear they will be weak and end up forgotten. As a result each norn seeks to prove themselves, to build their legend through feats of individual valour and great victories. Every norn hopes their legend will be told by the skaald and celebrated at a moot with story and song. Norn usually have a great sense of personal honour, for a norn without respect from others is already forgotten. For many norn, the focus on prestige means they're willing to accept any challenge which will prove their worth. They will never give up on their battle, quarry or friends, even if it means years spent tackling a strong enemy or difficult task. Yet for other norn the pursuit of prestige translates to bragging, bullying, unwise snap decisions and an unwillingness to listen to reason.

Norn are a hearty, proud people—quick to anger, but also to forgive. They are, by their nature, a race of optimists, keeping a positive outlook even though they have been driven from their homelands. A setback is seen as just another challenge to overcome. They respect those who can best them in a fight so brawling among norn is common. Drinking alcohol, often to excess, is also a large part of norn culture. Being drunk and fighting is not an uncommon combination, for example, norn alemoots feature competitions of 'skill' which require the competitors to be very drunk to compete.

A norn might proudly trace their worthy ancestors through many generations and may pass a common name through a family (such as Olaf Olafson, seventh son of Olaf) but a great ancestry and family connections do not grant automatic respect to a norn. Everyone is judged not by their lineage or associated group but by what deeds they have done. There is no such thing as infamy to the norn; they are often tolerant of an individual to whom other races might treat with hostility by association.

entries.name: Bear
entries: As the Spirit of ferocity and confidence and the natural embodiment of all bears, Bear is the most revered of all the Spirits of the Wild and is said to be an icon of strength, insight, and wisdom. According to ancient myths, she bestowed the norn with the totem ability to Become the Bear. The norn view Bear as both a Mother who protects her cubs and a Sister who helps people find their inner strength. She is said to give norn the power to face all odds.

entries.name: Owl
entries: As the natural embodiment of all owls, Owl was revered as one of the Spirits of the Wild among the norn. Although she was a gentle Spirit who embodied home and hearth, she was also a silent and clever predator with ambush-like swiftness and precision. Helping the sick and wounded pleased her. Owl put her family's needs and safety above all else, often teaching others that the purpose of the hunt was not just to kill but also to provide and protect. Tales about Owl depict her offering her wisdom to both norn and her fellow Spirits of the Wild.

entries.name: Raven
entries: As the Spirit of knowledge and foresight and the natural embodiment of ravens, Raven is said to be a trickster who is fond of riddles and wordplay and likes to test the wits of his followers and anyone else who wishes to uncover his secrets. He calls his followers fledglings, inspires artists, and also favours acts of kindness towards ravens who are viewed as his flock. In some cases Raven is believed to personally come to take the life of a norn who was about to die and guide the soul to the Mists.

The norn believe that Raven sees and knows all, and requires nothing but the petitioner's promise to always seek wisdom to grant them his blessing of swiftness. He only views someone unworthy in his eyes if the person does not learn from their mistakes, and he rewards the clever, the sincere, and those who seek out new experiences such as traveling the world. Although Raven represents the facets of mind that grant beings the vision to see and the wisdom to understand, his teachings also contain a darker side which some of his wayward shamans would cruelly embrace in their quest for knowledge and power at the expense of others.

entries.name: Snow Leopard
entries: Snow Leopard, also known as Spirit of Snow Leopard or Snow Leopard Spirit, is a Great Spirit of the Wild and the natural embodiment of all snow leopards. She is revered by the norn, who build shrines and lodges to honor her, the most notable being the Snow Leopard Lodge. According to her Speakers, Snow Leopard is dedicated to stealth and prowess on the hunt, as well as independence. She reminds us that sometimes the best approach is the one least expected. She is also associated with strategy, laughter in the face of danger, and is considered to be solitary.

entries.name: Wolf
entries: As the Spirit of teamwork, friendship, family, and the natural embodiment of all wolves, Wolf is said to be dedicated to brotherhood as he imparts the virtues of loyalty, ferocity, and strength in numbers. He is revered as a masculine spirit, and the norn call him their brother, son, father, and uncle when they ask for his blessing. According to old tales, Owl taught Wolf how his hunting would be for naught if his family dined alone, making Wolf heed Owl's wisdom as he returned to his cubs. Although in some cases Raven was believed to personally come to take the life of a norn who was about to die and guide the soul to the Mists, Wolf was also known to do the same to people who walked in his footsteps.
